skill.name: research-driven-planning
skill.description: Guides research-backed project planning through specification, evidence collection, structured plans, and repeated pre-mortem risk review.
skill.summary: Create research-backed implementation plans with structured risk analysis before development starts.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or destructive behavior. The high static findings for C2 and weak cryptography are false positives from planning terms and diagram labels. The skill does document shell commands, npx usage, and file reads and writes, so publication should include a clear execution and filesystem warning.

medium_findings.title: Documented External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill instructs users to run shell commands, npx claude-flow commands, jq, bc, and node scripts. The usage appears task-related and not malicious, but it can execute downloaded packages or local scripts in the user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit executable shell examples. The commands are visible and aligned with the planning workflow, so risk is operational rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Project Artifact File Reads and Writes
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill reads SPEC.md, plan files, and .claude artifacts, then writes enhanced plans, reports, and hidden .claude artifacts. This is expected for the workflow, but it can persist sensitive planning details and overwrite project files if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Node.js snippets directly use fs.readFileSync and fs.writeFileSync on project artifacts. The paths are local and workflow-related, with no evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Keyword Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static C2 and weak cryptography matches occur in documentation and a DOT workflow diagram. The reviewed lines describe memory namespaces, failure confidence, pre-mortem analysis, and planning status, not command-and-control logic or cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines are prose, diagram labels, or planning thresholds. No cryptographic implementation, beaconing, persistence, or C2 endpoint is present.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE CHECK: Targeted review found no text claiming system authority, approval status, or instructions to bypass security analysis. The file status line says production-ready, but it describes the skill version and does not instruct evaluators to skip review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The checked text is ordinary documentation metadata. No suspicious override, role reassignment, or analysis-bypass language was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Workflow Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples pass file contents through shell substitution such as cat and jq. This is useful for memory storage and reports, but users should review generated files before running these snippets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The substitution syntax is directly present. It operates on local planning files, so the concern is accidental disclosure or malformed input rather than confirmed abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Claude Artifact Directory Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow stores and reads planning outputs under .claude/.artifacts. Hidden directories can be easy to overlook during review and may contain sensitive project planning data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden path is explicitly used many times. It is a legitimate local artifact directory, but sensitive contents may persist there.

content.value_statement: Complex projects often start with weak requirements, untested assumptions, and hidden failure modes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through structured research, planning, and pre-mortem review before implementation.
actual_capabilities: Creates a structured project specification from goals, requirements, constraints, and success criteria.
Coordinates separate research perspectives for technology, quality, security, and domain evidence.
Transforms a specification into a task plan enriched with research findings and known risks.
Runs repeated pre-mortem review patterns to identify failure modes and mitigation strategies.
Packages planning outputs for a later implementation workflow and records memory namespaces.
Provides troubleshooting steps for low-quality research, poor convergence, and integration failures.
limitations: It is designed for complex projects and can be too slow for small fixes.
It depends on external Claude Flow commands and local artifact files when users run the examples.
Research quality depends on available sources and the model performing the research.
Risk percentages are planning estimates, not formal reliability guarantees.

use_cases.title: Scope a New Product Feature
use_cases.description: Turn a feature idea into requirements, research questions, risks, and a structured implementation plan.

use_cases.title: Choose a Technical Approach
use_cases.description: Compare libraries or architecture options using evidence, security history, maintainability, and production fit.

use_cases.title: Prepare High-Risk Delivery Work
use_cases.description: Identify likely failures before coding and add mitigations to the implementation plan.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a clear project plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use research-driven-planning for this feature: [describe feature]. Capture requirements, constraints, success criteria, risks, and a first implementation plan.

prompt_templates.title: Research Technology Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use research-driven-planning to compare [option A], [option B], and [option C] for [project]. Include evidence, security concerns, maintenance risk, and a recommendation.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Pre-Mortem
prompt_templates.scenario: Test a draft plan for likely failures.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use research-driven-planning to review this plan: [paste plan]. Identify failure modes, root causes, mitigation strategies, and remaining confidence risks.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Loop Two Handoff
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced handoff from planning to implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use research-driven-planning to produce a Loop 1 handoff for [project]. Include SPEC summary, research evidence, enhanced task plan, risk registry, mitigations, and implementation dependencies.

output_examples.input: Plan an authentication system for an Express API with PostgreSQL and two-factor authentication.
output: A specification covering authentication flows, role access, password reset, performance targets, and security requirements.
A research summary comparing token storage, session design, two-factor options, and library choices.
A risk register covering account takeover, token leakage, lockout abuse, and migration errors.

output_examples.input: Review a proposed migration from a monolith to a service-based architecture.
output: A staged plan that separates discovery, service boundaries, data ownership, rollout, and rollback work.
A pre-mortem summary for distributed tracing, data consistency, deployment coupling, and operational readiness.
A handoff package with tasks, dependencies, evidence, and mitigation owners.

best_practices: Use it before coding starts, when requirements and architecture choices still can change.
Review generated shell commands and artifact paths before running them in a real repository.
Provide concrete constraints, acceptance criteria, and known risks to improve research quality.
anti_patterns: Do not use it for urgent hotfixes where a small direct change is safer.
Do not treat estimated failure confidence as a formal guarantee.
Do not store secrets, private customer data, or credentials in planning artifacts.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces a specification, research synthesis, enhanced plan, risk analysis, and implementation handoff package.

faq.question: Does it write code?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on research, planning, and risk review before implementation begins.

faq.question: Why does it use shell commands?
faq.answer: The commands demonstrate how to store planning artifacts, run local scripts, and validate generated outputs.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because users may run external commands and create local artifacts.

faq.question: Can it handle small tasks?
faq.answer: It can, but the process is usually too heavy for trivial fixes or well-understood changes.

faq.question: What should users avoid storing?
faq.answer: Users should avoid credentials, private customer data, production secrets, and regulated data in planning files.
